subtopic_detail: Discriminating cannabis-induced psychotic disorder from first-episode primary psychosis
stem: You are the registrar in the early psychosis service reviewing a 19-year-old man who is 7 days post-discharge from an inpatient unit following a first presentation with persecutory delusions, auditory hallucinations and disorganised thinking. Symptoms began acutely 4 weeks ago after heavy daily cannabis use over 6 months. He has been abstinent from cannabis for 14 days under family supervision. Olanzapine 10 mg nightly was started during admission with marked improvement.

data_table.Mental state: Euthymic affect, persecutory ideas now overvalued not fixed, no hallucinations, fully oriented
data_table.Family history: Paternal cousin with schizophrenia, maternal grandmother with depression
data_table.Substance history: Daily high-potency cannabis 0.5 to 1 g for 6 months, no other substances, no withdrawal
data_table.Medical workup: Urine drug screen positive cannabis at admission, repeat now negative; CT brain normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan now?

options.text: Stop olanzapine immediately as it is no longer required following discharge from inpatient unit
options.rationale: Even substance-induced psychotic episodes typically need antipsychotic continuation for several months and abrupt cessation risks relapse. (Source: RANZCP psychosis guideline)

options.text: Refer for a Mental Health Act community treatment order to enforce ongoing antipsychotic medication
options.rationale: Voluntary engagement with family support is appropriate; a CTO is not justified in a cooperative young person with insight. (Source: RANZCP psychosis guideline)

options.text: Switch olanzapine to amisulpride to avoid future metabolic risk and review at 6 months only
options.rationale: Switching agents during a successful response is unnecessary; metabolic monitoring with continued olanzapine is the standard initial path. (Source: RANZCP psychosis guideline)

options.text: Continue olanzapine for at least 6 months with brief structured cannabis use intervention and headspace follow-up
options.rationale: Australian early psychosis guidance recommends maintaining antipsychotic for at least 6 to 12 months after first-episode psychosis with intensive cannabis cessation support; persistence of symptoms beyond 1 month off cannabis would reclassify the disorder. (Source: RANZCP psychosis guideline)

options.text: Provide reassurance and discharge to the GP for a single follow-up appointment in 3 months
options.rationale: Discharge without intensive early-psychosis follow-up loses the highest-impact window of intervention. (Source: RANZCP psychosis guideline)

explanation.summary: Cannabis-induced psychotic disorder shares features with first-episode primary psychosis, but discrimination requires longitudinal observation. DSM-5 substance-induced psychotic disorder requires psychotic symptoms during or within 1 month of intoxication or withdrawal AND attribution beyond mere co-occurrence. If psychotic symptoms persist for more than 1 month after substance cessation, a primary psychotic disorder diagnosis is favoured. Even in confirmed substance-induced episodes, approximately 30 to 50 percent transition to a primary psychotic disorder within 3 years, especially with heavy use of high-potency cannabis from a young age and a family history. Early psychosis services provide intensive support during this critical window.
key_points: Persistence of psychosis more than 1 month after cessation suggests primary disorder
Approximately 30 to 50 percent transition to primary psychosis within 3 years
High-potency cannabis and youth amplify transition risk
Continue antipsychotic at least 6 to 12 months after first episode
Intensive early psychosis service and substance support reduce relapse
explanation.pearls: Heavy cannabis use before age 15 confers roughly four-fold increased risk of adult schizophrenia in cohort studies, with the COMT Val/Val polymorphism implicated in mediating risk.

subtopic_detail: Recognition of GHB overdose presenting with rapid fluctuation in conscious state
stem: You are the emergency registrar reviewing a 23-year-old woman brought in by ambulance from a music festival. She was found unresponsive in a portable toilet and on arrival has fluctuating GCS rapidly between 4 and 12 over 20 minutes. She has no signs of trauma, miosis, or pupillary asymmetry. Her vital signs are stable. Bystanders saw no injectables and no prescription containers. She has woken briefly, become agitated, and then dropped GCS again twice.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.6, HR 64, BP 110/70 mmHg, RR 12, SpO2 99 percent on 4 L oxygen via Hudson
data_table.Examination: No trauma, pupils 4 mm reactive, no needle tracks, no neck rigidity
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.36, lactate 1.8, glucose 5.4 mmol/L, paracetamol and salicylate negative, ethanol 0.04
data_table.Urine toxicology: Pending; rapid amphetamine and opioid panel negative

lead_in: Which is the most likely cause of her presentation?

options.text: Acute opioid overdose despite negative urine drug screen and absence of typical examination signs
options.rationale: Opioid overdose produces persistent miosis and respiratory depression rather than rapid swings between alertness and unconsciousness. (Source: NSW Toxicology Service)

options.text: Gamma-hydroxybutyrate intoxication producing characteristic rapid fluctuation in conscious state
options.rationale: GHB intoxication classically produces rapid swings between deep coma and combative wakefulness over short periods, typically resolving within 4 to 6 hours, and is undetectable on standard urine panels. (Source: NSW Toxicology Service)

options.text: MDMA toxicity with serotonin syndrome accounting for the presentation and observed symptoms
options.rationale: MDMA toxicity produces hyperthermia, hypertension, hyponatraemia, and serotonin syndrome features rather than coma fluctuation. (Source: NSW Toxicology Service)

options.text: Acute methamphetamine intoxication producing rapid swings between agitation and unresponsiveness
options.rationale: Methamphetamine intoxication produces sustained sympathomimetic features (tachycardia, hypertension, hyperthermia) rather than reduced GCS. (Source: NSW Toxicology Service)

options.text: Cannabis intoxication producing acute psychosis with associated rapid fluctuation in conscious state
options.rationale: Cannabis intoxication rarely depresses GCS in healthy young adults and does not produce minute-to-minute fluctuation. (Source: NSW Toxicology Service)

explanation.summary: Gamma-hydroxybutyrate (GHB) and the pro-drugs GBL and 1,4-butanediol are colourless, odourless liquids commonly used at festivals. Intoxication is characterised by rapid fluctuation between deep coma and combative wakefulness over a 4 to 6 hour course. Bradycardia, mild respiratory depression, and vomiting are common. Standard urine drug screens do not detect GHB and the diagnosis is clinical. Management is supportive with airway protection, lateral positioning, oxygen and observation; intubation is reserved for sustained loss of airway reflexes or aspiration. Withdrawal in dependent users is dangerous and resembles severe alcohol withdrawal with delirium and seizures; treatment is with high-dose benzodiazepines.
key_points: Rapid fluctuation between coma and arousal is the hallmark of GHB
Effects resolve within 4 to 6 hours with supportive care
GHB is not detected on standard urine drug screens
Co-ingestion (alcohol, benzodiazepines) prolongs and deepens coma
GHB withdrawal needs benzodiazepines and may need ICU
explanation.pearls: Flumazenil and naloxone do not reverse GHB intoxication and giving them risks precipitating mixed benzodiazepine or opioid withdrawal when there has been co-ingestion.

subtopic_detail: Discriminating functional neurological disorder from malingering and factitious disorder
stem: You are the neurology registrar consulted on a 38-year-old woman with sudden right-sided weakness on awakening 5 days ago. There was no headache, dysphasia, or facial droop. MRI brain and MR angiography are normal. Examination shows positive Hoover sign on the right leg, sudden weakness that reverses with distraction, and intact reflexes. She is distressed by the symptoms, has missed work, and is requesting a second neurological opinion. There is no ongoing legal claim and no external secondary gain identified.

data_table.Examination: Hoover sign positive, give-way weakness in right upper and lower limb, deep tendon reflexes 2+ symmetric, no clonus
data_table.Investigations: MRI brain, MR angiography, glucose, electrolytes, CK all normal
data_table.Psychosocial: Distressed by symptoms, no compensation claim, no military or insurance context
data_table.Behaviour: Cooperative with examination, willing to engage with physiotherapy

lead_in: What is the most appropriate diagnosis and next step?

options.text: Malingering: discharge with safety-net advice and no further follow-up needed at present
options.rationale: Malingering requires an identifiable external incentive (avoiding work, compensation, criminal proceedings), which is absent here. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Factitious disorder imposed on self: refer for psychiatric management and avoid further investigation
options.rationale: Factitious disorder requires intentional deception to assume the sick role; willingness to engage with physiotherapy and distress about symptoms argues against this. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Conversion disorder: order CT angiogram of the carotids to rule out a missed structural cause
options.rationale: Further imaging is not indicated when MRI and MR angiography are already normal and positive functional signs are present; FND is a rule-in diagnosis. (Source: ANZAN FND consensus)

options.text: Stroke mimic: admit for observation on stroke unit with 24-hour neurological observations performed
options.rationale: FND with positive signs and normal imaging does not require stroke unit admission. (Source: ANZAN FND consensus)

options.text: Functional neurological disorder: explain diagnosis using positive signs and refer for specialist FND physiotherapy
options.rationale: FND is now a positive rule-in diagnosis based on internal inconsistency signs (Hoover, distractibility), with neurologist-led explanation and specialist FND physiotherapy as the evidence-based first step. (Source: ANZAN FND consensus)

explanation.summary: DSM-5 frames functional neurological symptom disorder (conversion disorder) as a positive rule-in diagnosis: incompatibility between symptoms and recognised neurological disease, plus internal inconsistency on examination such as Hoover sign, hip abductor sign, drift without pronation, or distractibility. Malingering and factitious disorder both require intentional symptom production; malingering for external incentive, factitious to assume the sick role. Most patients with FND have neither; they have genuine, distressing, involuntary symptoms. Management is led by neurology with positive explanation of the diagnosis using demonstration of the signs to the patient, structured physiotherapy with FND-trained therapists, and CBT or psychiatric input when comorbid depression or trauma is present.
key_points: FND is a positive rule-in diagnosis with internal inconsistency signs
Hoover sign and distractibility have high specificity for FND
Malingering requires identifiable external incentive
Factitious disorder requires intentional deception for sick role
Specialist FND physiotherapy is evidence-based first treatment
explanation.pearls: Demonstrating Hoover sign to the patient at the bedside as part of the diagnostic explanation produces a higher rate of acceptance and engagement with FND physiotherapy than the explanation alone.

subtopic_detail: Empiric antibiotic choice for suspected prosthetic valve endocarditis
stem: You are the medical registrar reviewing a 64-year-old man with 3 weeks of fevers, weight loss, and night sweats. He had a mechanical aortic valve replacement 14 months ago. Two sets of blood cultures taken 30 minutes apart are pending. His observations are stable and there is no peripheral stigmata of endocarditis. A transthoracic echocardiogram shows a possible 6 mm vegetation on the prosthetic aortic valve and trivial regurgitation. He has no penicillin allergy.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.6, HR 96, BP 124/68 mmHg, SpO2 98 percent
data_table.Bloods: WCC 12.4 x10^9/L, CRP 88 mg/L, creatinine 92 micromol/L
data_table.Examination: Mechanical second heart sound, no peripheral stigmata, no embolic phenomena
data_table.Imaging: TTE: possible 6 mm vegetation on prosthetic aortic valve, trivial AR, normal LV function

lead_in: Which empiric antibiotic regimen is most appropriate?

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin 1.8 g 4-hourly plus gentamicin 1 mg/kg 8-hourly as initial therapy
options.rationale: Penicillin and gentamicin alone do not cover staphylococcal species (including methicillin-resistant strains) that are common in prosthetic valve endocarditis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous flucloxacillin 2 g 4-hourly plus benzylpenicillin 1.8 g 4-hourly plus gentamicin
options.rationale: This regimen covers native valve endocarditis but does not address methicillin-resistant staphylococci or coagulase-negative staphylococci common on prostheses. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin plus gentamicin plus rifampicin pending blood culture and susceptibility
options.rationale: Late prosthetic valve endocarditis requires empiric vancomycin (MRSA and coagulase-negative staphylococci), gentamicin (synergy), and rifampicin (biofilm penetration) until pathogen is known. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g daily monotherapy until blood cultures result and adjust thereafter
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone monotherapy does not adequately cover staphylococci including MRSA. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral linezolid 600 mg twice daily monotherapy with outpatient transthoracic review in 1 week
options.rationale: Oral monotherapy is inappropriate for prosthetic valve endocarditis; intravenous combination is mandatory. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Infective endocarditis empiric therapy is stratified by valve type and risk. For native valve endocarditis benzylpenicillin plus flucloxacillin plus gentamicin is standard; vancomycin replaces benzylpenicillin and flucloxacillin in severe penicillin allergy or suspected MRSA. For prosthetic valve endocarditis later than 12 months post-surgery the staphylococcal risk plus biofilm formation justifies vancomycin plus gentamicin plus rifampicin. Early prosthetic valve endocarditis (within 12 months) is dominated by nosocomial pathogens and uses the same regimen but with consideration of fungi. Three sets of blood cultures from separate sites and transoesophageal echo confirm the diagnosis. Surgical referral is indicated for prosthetic involvement, large vegetations, embolic events, heart failure, or perivalvular abscess.
key_points: Three blood culture sets over time before antibiotics where possible
Native valve empiric: benzylpenicillin plus flucloxacillin plus gentamicin
Prosthetic valve empiric: vancomycin plus gentamicin plus rifampicin
TOE outperforms TTE for prosthetic valves and complications
Surgical referral for prosthesis, HF, abscess, large vegetation, persistent bacteraemia
explanation.pearls: Rifampicin is added for its biofilm penetration on prosthetic material, but never given alone for staphylococci because of rapid resistance emergence.

subtopic_detail: Application of the sepsis hour-1 bundle in suspected community-acquired sepsis
stem: You are the emergency registrar reviewing a 58-year-old woman with 24 hours of dysuria, loin pain, and rigors. She presents looking unwell, mottled peripheries, capillary refill 3 seconds. Her observations on triage 15 minutes ago triggered an emergency response, and no antibiotics or fluids have yet been given. She is fully alert. She has no documented antibiotic allergy and her usual medications are amlodipine and metformin.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.1, HR 124, BP 86/52 mmHg, RR 24, SpO2 95 percent room air
data_table.Bloods: WCC 18.6 x10^9/L, lactate 4.2 mmol/L, creatinine 168 micromol/L (baseline 90)
data_table.Examination: Tender right flank, no peritonism, peripheries mottled, mentation intact

lead_in: Within the first hour, which action is most important to perform first?

options.text: Administer empiric intravenous gentamicin and ampicillin within 1 hour after taking blood cultures
options.rationale: The sepsis hour-1 bundle prioritises lactate, blood cultures before antibiotics, broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour, and 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate above 4 mmol/L; this regimen targets pyelonephritis-related sepsis per Australian guidance. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; Surviving Sepsis Campaign)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; Surviving Sepsis Campaign

options.text: Order computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis before any further escalation or therapy
options.rationale: Imaging is important for source control but must not delay the hour-1 antibiotic and fluid bundle. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; Surviving Sepsis Campaign)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; Surviving Sepsis Campaign

options.text: Wait for urine culture susceptibility results before commencing any empiric antibiotic therapy
options.rationale: Waiting hours for susceptibilities is unsafe and contravenes the hour-1 bundle. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; Surviving Sepsis Campaign)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; Surviving Sepsis Campaign

options.text: Commence intravenous noradrenaline immediately at 0.1 microgram/kg/min as first-line resuscitation
options.rationale: Vasopressor is used when MAP remains under 65 mmHg after 30 mL/kg crystalloid; fluid resuscitation is the initial response unless contraindicated. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; Surviving Sepsis Campaign)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; Surviving Sepsis Campaign

options.text: Provide 1 L of 5 percent dextrose intravenously as bolus over 30 minutes for volume expansion
options.rationale: Dextrose is not a resuscitation fluid; balanced crystalloid (Hartmann or Plasmalyte) is preferred for septic shock. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; Surviving Sepsis Campaign)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; Surviving Sepsis Campaign

explanation.summary: The Surviving Sepsis Campaign hour-1 bundle integrates measure lactate, obtain blood cultures before antibiotics, administer broad-spectrum antibiotics, begin rapid administration of 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid for hypotension or lactate at or above 4 mmol/L, and start vasopressors during or after fluid resuscitation if MAP remains below 65 mmHg. For community-acquired pyelonephritis with sepsis, Australian Therapeutic Guidelines recommend ampicillin plus gentamicin (with renal function adjusted dosing). Source control (urological intervention for obstructive uropathy, surgical debridement, drainage) is sought as soon as possible after stabilisation.
key_points: Hour-1 bundle: lactate, cultures, antibiotics, fluids, vasopressors
30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid for hypotension or lactate >= 4
Vasopressor if MAP under 65 mmHg after fluid
Source control as early as feasible after resuscitation
Ampicillin plus gentamicin is first-line for pyelonephritis sepsis
explanation.pearls: Single-dose gentamicin 5 to 7 mg/kg followed by re-dosing only after a 24-hour level minimises nephrotoxicity while achieving high peak-MIC ratios in the first 24 hours of sepsis.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; Surviving Sepsis Campaign

subtopic_detail: Recognition and surgical urgency of necrotising fasciitis using clinical and LRINEC features
stem: You are the surgical registrar called to the emergency department to review a 56-year-old man with type 2 diabetes who has 18 hours of severe pain in the right leg out of proportion to the limited erythema. He has had two previous episodes of cellulitis treated with oral antibiotics. He is increasingly distressed by the pain and his observations are deteriorating. There is a small skin break over the shin from a gardening injury 2 days ago.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.0, HR 130, BP 92/58 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 96 percent
data_table.Bloods: WCC 22 x10^9/L, sodium 132 mmol/L, glucose 16 mmol/L, CRP 220 mg/L, creatinine 152 micromol/L, haemoglobin 132 g/L
data_table.Examination: Localised erythema 5 cm proximal to ankle, exquisite tenderness extending 15 cm proximally, no crepitus, no bullae yet
data_table.Imaging: Plain radiograph shows no soft tissue gas

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence oral cephalexin 500 mg every 6 hours and review the response in the emergency department in 2 hours
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics do not cover the spectrum of necrotising soft tissue infection and would dangerously delay surgical debridement. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Order urgent magnetic resonance imaging before any decision on surgical exploration is made
options.rationale: Imaging should never delay surgical exploration in suspected necrotising fasciitis; clinical features and LRINEC score are sufficient to act. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Refer for orthopaedic review next day to plan elective wound debridement in the operating room
options.rationale: Delaying debridement beyond hours in necrotising fasciitis substantially increases mortality. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Activate emergency operating theatre booking for surgical exploration plus empiric meropenem and clindamycin
options.rationale: Pain out of proportion to skin findings, systemic toxicity, and an LRINEC score of 8 or more (his is 11) demand emergency surgical exploration with empiric broad-spectrum cover plus clindamycin for toxin suppression. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Admit for observation on the medical ward and intravenous flucloxacillin alone for severe cellulitis
options.rationale: Flucloxacillin alone does not cover gram-negatives or anaerobes and is insufficient for necrotising fasciitis; surgery is required. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Necrotising fasciitis is a surgical emergency where rapid spreading subcutaneous and fascial necrosis develops faster than overlying skin changes can keep up. The hallmark is pain out of proportion to examination, systemic toxicity, and rapidly evolving signs (oedema, bullae, crepitus, skin necrosis, anaesthesia over the affected area). The LRINEC score uses CRP, white cell count, haemoglobin, sodium, creatinine, and glucose to risk stratify: score 6 or more is intermediate to high risk, 8 or more confirms high risk. Definitive treatment is urgent surgical debridement; empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics include a carbapenem (or piperacillin-tazobactam), vancomycin or linezolid, plus clindamycin for toxin suppression. Repeated debridement at 24 to 48 hours is the norm.
key_points: Pain out of proportion to skin findings is the key early clue
LRINEC score 6 or more raises suspicion; 8 or more confirms it
Surgical debridement within hours saves life
Empiric antibiotics: meropenem + vancomycin + clindamycin
Repeated debridement and ICU support are usually needed
explanation.pearls: Clindamycin is added specifically to inhibit streptococcal exotoxin and panton-valentine leukocidin production, distinct from its antibacterial effect.

subtopic_detail: Treatment selection after minimal trauma vertebral fracture in postmenopausal woman
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 72-year-old postmenopausal woman 6 weeks after a thoracic vertebral compression fracture sustained when she stepped down from a kerb. She has had no other fractures. She takes alendronate 70 mg weekly correctly for 5 years and a daily calcium and vitamin D supplement. Recent investigations are back. Her renal function is preserved and there is no history of oesophageal disease.

data_table.DXA: Lumbar spine T score -3.2, femoral neck T score -2.9, total hip -2.7
data_table.Bloods: 25-OH vitamin D 78 nmol/L, corrected calcium 2.32 mmol/L, eGFR 78 mL/min/1.73m^2, TSH 1.4 mIU/L
data_table.Other: PTH 4.6 pmol/L, normal SPEP, no malignant features

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next change in management?

options.text: Continue current alendronate at the same dose and review again with repeat DXA in 2 years
options.rationale: Minimal trauma vertebral fracture on alendronate represents treatment failure and demands escalation. (Source: RACGP: Osteoporosis)

options.text: Switch alendronate to teriparatide 20 mcg subcutaneously daily for 18 months under specialist review
options.rationale: An incident minimal trauma vertebral fracture despite oral bisphosphonate is an indication for anabolic therapy with teriparatide; PBS subsidy applies under specialist authority. (Source: RACGP: Osteoporosis)

options.text: Add daily high-dose calcitriol 0.5 micrograms to current alendronate to improve fracture healing
options.rationale: Calcitriol is reserved for renal osteodystrophy; it does not address established bisphosphonate failure. (Source: RACGP: Osteoporosis)

options.text: Switch alendronate to oral risedronate 35 mg weekly as an alternative oral bisphosphonate option
options.rationale: Switching between oral bisphosphonates does not address treatment failure; a different drug class is required. (Source: RACGP: Osteoporosis)

options.text: Switch alendronate to raloxifene 60 mg daily as a selective oestrogen receptor modulator
options.rationale: Raloxifene reduces vertebral but not hip or non-vertebral fracture and is not appropriate as escalation after vertebral fracture on a bisphosphonate. (Source: RACGP: Osteoporosis)

explanation.summary: Osteoporosis treatment in Australia is structured by PBS criteria: minimal trauma vertebral or hip fracture, age 70 or above with T score -2.5 or lower, or prolonged corticosteroid therapy. First line is oral bisphosphonate (alendronate or risedronate) or intravenous zoledronic acid annually; denosumab 60 mg subcutaneously 6-monthly is an alternative, especially in renal impairment. An incident fracture or further bone density loss while on antiresorptive therapy indicates treatment failure and consideration of anabolic therapy. Teriparatide is approved for 18 months and is followed by an antiresorptive agent (denosumab or bisphosphonate) to preserve gains. Compliance, calcium and vitamin D status, and secondary causes must be reviewed.
key_points: Minimal trauma fracture despite bisphosphonate equals treatment failure
Teriparatide 20 mcg subcutaneous daily for 18 months follows
An antiresorptive agent must follow teriparatide to preserve gains
Adherence and secondary causes must be reviewed
Denosumab is an alternative escalation in renal impairment
explanation.pearls: Teriparatide is contraindicated when there is a previous skeletal radiotherapy, hypercalcaemia, Paget disease, or open epiphyses; specialist review is required for PBS authority.

sources.label: Healthy Bones Australia: Clinical practice guideline

subtopic_detail: Workup for suspected acute HIV seroconversion in a man with febrile illness and mucosal ulcers
stem: You are the registrar in a sexual health clinic reviewing a 26-year-old man with 10 days of fevers, sore throat, malaise, painful oral ulcers, a generalised maculopapular rash on the trunk, and tender cervical lymphadenopathy. He had condomless receptive anal sex with a new partner 5 weeks ago. He has not previously been on pre-exposure prophylaxis. His last HIV test was negative 6 months ago.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.4, HR 96, BP 118/70 mmHg, SpO2 98 percent
data_table.Bloods: WCC 4.2 x10^9/L (lymphopenia), platelets 116 x10^9/L, ALT 88 U/L, CRP 32 mg/L
data_table.Examination: Oropharyngeal ulcers, tender bilateral posterior cervical lymph nodes, truncal maculopapular rash, no genital ulcers
data_table.STI history: Last negative HIV 4th generation test 6 months ago, no PrEP, single recent exposure

lead_in: Which test most reliably establishes acute HIV infection at this stage?

options.text: Standard 3rd generation HIV antibody test from a venous blood sample as the first investigation
options.rationale: 3rd generation tests detect antibodies only and have a 3 to 6 week window; they may be negative in seroconversion. (Source: ASHM: HIV testing policy)

options.text: Rapid point-of-care fingerprick antibody test as a definitive single test for acute infection
options.rationale: Point-of-care antibody-only tests perform worst in the seroconversion window and may miss acute infection. (Source: ASHM: HIV testing policy)

options.text: Anti-HIV western blot performed on a serum sample collected today to confirm acute infection
options.rationale: Western blot is a confirmatory antibody test and is also negative during seroconversion windows. (Source: ASHM: HIV testing policy)

options.text: Quantitative chest computed tomography to assess for opportunistic infection complicating seroconversion
options.rationale: Imaging does not detect HIV infection; this question is about diagnostic confirmation. (Source: ASHM: HIV testing policy)

options.text: HIV RNA viral load polymerase chain reaction alongside a 4th generation antigen antibody combination test
options.rationale: In suspected acute seroconversion (less than 28 days), HIV RNA PCR detects virus before antibody seroconversion; the 4th generation Ag/Ab test adds p24 antigen detection. Both establish the diagnosis confidently. (Source: ASHM: HIV testing policy)

explanation.summary: Acute HIV seroconversion classically presents with a mononucleosis-like illness 2 to 4 weeks after high-risk exposure: fever, pharyngitis, lymphadenopathy, rash, oral or genital ulcers, with lymphopenia, raised transaminases, and atypical lymphocytes. Diagnostically, HIV RNA viral load detects virus 1 to 2 weeks post-exposure; p24 antigen detected by 4th generation tests becomes positive about 2 weeks post-exposure; antibodies seroconvert at 3 to 6 weeks. A combination of HIV RNA PCR and 4th generation Ag/Ab test maximises sensitivity in the window. Confirmed acute infection requires immediate referral to an s100-prescribing HIV physician for early initiation of antiretroviral therapy, contact tracing, and counselling.
key_points: Acute HIV mimics infectious mononucleosis at 2 to 4 weeks post-exposure
HIV RNA PCR turns positive within 1 to 2 weeks
4th generation Ag/Ab adds p24 antigen detection at ~2 weeks
Antibody-only tests are unreliable in the window period
Immediate referral and rapid ART start are recommended
explanation.pearls: Same-day ART initiation in acute HIV reduces transmission and progression, and is the standard of care at Australian sexual health centres; the rapid test result is no longer required before starting therapy in suspected acute disease.

subtopic_detail: Diagnostic sequence for suspected Cushing syndrome in a woman with central obesity and hypertension
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 42-year-old woman with 18 months of progressive central weight gain, easy bruising, proximal muscle weakness in climbing stairs, and new hypertension. She does not take exogenous corticosteroids and denies herbal preparations. She has type 2 diabetes diagnosed last year and her BP is 158/96 mmHg on amlodipine. Her menstrual cycles have become irregular and acne has worsened.

data_table.Examination: Central obesity, supraclavicular fat pads, dorsocervical fat pad, violaceous abdominal striae, proximal hip and shoulder weakness
data_table.Bloods: HbA1c 7.6 percent, potassium 3.4 mmol/L, fasting glucose 8.4 mmol/L
data_table.Medications: Amlodipine 10 mg daily, metformin 1 g twice daily; no glucocorticoids or herbal supplements

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic test?

options.text: Random serum cortisol level taken between 08:00 and 10:00 as the first screening investigation
options.rationale: Random cortisol levels are too variable and do not establish or exclude Cushing syndrome. (Source: Endocrine Society Cushing syndrome guideline)

options.text: Immediate adrenal computed tomography to identify a primary adrenal source of hypercortisolism
options.rationale: Imaging without biochemical confirmation risks discovering incidentalomas and missing pituitary or ectopic sources. (Source: Endocrine Society Cushing syndrome guideline)

options.text: Overnight 1 mg dexamethasone suppression test or late-night salivary cortisol or 24-hour urinary free cortisol
options.rationale: Any one of these three tests is acceptable as a first-line screen; confirmation requires two abnormal screening tests of different types before further localisation. (Source: Endocrine Society Cushing syndrome guideline)

options.text: Serum adrenocorticotrophic hormone level alone as the first investigation to localise hypercortisolism
options.rationale: ACTH localises but does not establish hypercortisolism; it follows confirmed Cushing syndrome rather than precedes it. (Source: Endocrine Society Cushing syndrome guideline)

options.text: Inferior petrosal sinus sampling to localise an ACTH-secreting pituitary microadenoma at this stage
options.rationale: IPSS is reserved for distinguishing pituitary from ectopic ACTH after confirmed ACTH-dependent Cushing syndrome. (Source: Endocrine Society Cushing syndrome guideline)

explanation.summary: Cushing syndrome workup starts with confirmation of hypercortisolism using one of three first-line tests: overnight 1 mg dexamethasone suppression (failure to suppress below 50 nmol/L), late-night salivary cortisol (elevated), or 24-hour urinary free cortisol (raised above reference range). Two abnormal tests from different categories are required before proceeding. Once hypercortisolism is confirmed, ACTH is measured. Suppressed ACTH indicates adrenal cause (proceed to adrenal CT or MRI). Normal or raised ACTH indicates ACTH-dependent disease, evaluated next with high-dose dexamethasone suppression, MRI pituitary, then inferior petrosal sinus sampling if non-diagnostic. Treatment is directed at the source: transsphenoidal surgery for pituitary adenoma, adrenalectomy for adrenal source, or treatment of ectopic source.
key_points: First-line: 1 mg overnight DST, late-night salivary cortisol, or 24h UFC
Two abnormal screens of different types confirm hypercortisolism
ACTH then distinguishes ACTH-dependent from independent
MRI pituitary and IPSS localise ACTH-dependent disease
Adrenal imaging follows confirmed ACTH-independent disease
explanation.pearls: Late-night salivary cortisol has the highest sensitivity in the most common form (early ACTH-dependent disease) because the earliest defect is loss of the circadian nadir at midnight.

subtopic_detail: Recognition of Miller Fisher variant of Guillain-Barre syndrome in a returned traveller
stem: You are the medical registrar reviewing a 36-year-old man with 4 days of progressively worsening unsteady gait, double vision, and difficulty reading. He returned from Vietnam 3 weeks ago, where he had 24 hours of watery diarrhoea that resolved without treatment. He has no significant past medical history and takes no regular medications. He is fully alert with normal language and cognition.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7, HR 84, BP 122/74 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99 percent
data_table.Examination: Bilateral abducens palsy, broad-based ataxic gait, absent ankle and knee reflexes, normal limb power, sensation intact
data_table.Investigations: Pending CSF, normal CT brain, normal FBC and electrolytes, ECG normal
data_table.Travel and exposure: Returned 3 weeks ago from Vietnam; self-limiting gastroenteritis 4 weeks ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Order high-resolution MRI of the brainstem to investigate suspected acute disseminated encephalomyelitis
options.rationale: MRI may be done but the clinical triad of ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, and areflexia is diagnostic for Miller Fisher syndrome and demands immediate treatment. (Source: Australian and New Zealand Association of Neurologists; BMJ Best Practice GBS)
source_refs: Australian and New Zealand Association of Neurologists; BMJ Best Practice GBS

options.text: Send GQ1b antibody serology and provide reassurance pending the result with outpatient review
options.rationale: Anti-GQ1b is supportive but treatment should not await serology when the clinical picture is diagnostic. (Source: Australian and New Zealand Association of Neurologists; BMJ Best Practice GBS)
source_refs: Australian and New Zealand Association of Neurologists; BMJ Best Practice GBS

options.text: Commence high-dose oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 5 days as first-line therapy
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are not effective in Guillain-Barre or Miller Fisher and may worsen outcomes. (Source: Australian and New Zealand Association of Neurologists; BMJ Best Practice GBS)
source_refs: Australian and New Zealand Association of Neurologists; BMJ Best Practice GBS

options.text: Admit to a monitored bed with respiratory observation and commence IVIG 0.4 g/kg daily for 5 days
options.rationale: Miller Fisher syndrome is a Guillain-Barre variant treated with IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days with cardio-respiratory monitoring because of risk of bulbar and respiratory weakness progression. (Source: Australian and New Zealand Association of Neurologists; BMJ Best Practice GBS)
source_refs: Australian and New Zealand Association of Neurologists; BMJ Best Practice GBS

options.text: Order Tensilon test to differentiate from myasthenia gravis before any further treatment is started
options.rationale: Areflexia and ataxia are not features of myasthenia gravis; Tensilon test is not relevant here and is no longer routinely used. (Source: Australian and New Zealand Association of Neurologists; BMJ Best Practice GBS)
source_refs: Australian and New Zealand Association of Neurologists; BMJ Best Practice GBS

explanation.summary: Miller Fisher syndrome is the classic variant of Guillain-Barre syndrome characterised by the triad of ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, and areflexia, often preceded by Campylobacter jejuni gastroenteritis or upper respiratory infection. Anti-GQ1b antibodies are positive in over 85 percent of cases. CSF typically shows raised protein with normal cell count (albuminocytologic dissociation). Treatment is IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days or plasma exchange; corticosteroids are not effective and may worsen outcome. Respiratory monitoring with serial forced vital capacity is mandatory because of risk of progression to bulbar weakness and respiratory failure requiring intubation.
key_points: Triad of ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, areflexia is diagnostic
Anti-GQ1b antibodies are positive in over 85 percent of cases
CSF shows albuminocytologic dissociation
IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days or plasma exchange
Steroids are not effective and not recommended
explanation.pearls: A vital capacity below 20 mL/kg or rapidly falling on serial measurement is a stronger indicator of imminent respiratory failure in GBS than oxygen saturation, which falls late.

sources.label: Australian and New Zealand Association of Neurologists; BMJ Best Practice GBS
